content.text: Not every Staff engineer does the same kind of work. The title describes a level of seniority and scope of impact, but the day-to-day reality varies enormously depending on what an organization needs and what the individual does best. Four distinct archetypes — Tech Lead, Architect, Solver, and Right Hand — capture the dominant patterns that Staff-level engineers occupy in practice. Understanding these archetypes clarifies expectations, guides career planning, and helps engineering organizations place senior technical talent where it creates the most value.
What the Archetypes Are
The four archetypes were articulated by Will Larson in
Staff Engineer: Leadership Beyond the Management Track

(2021), drawing on interviews with hundreds of Staff-plus engineers across the industry. The archetypes are not job titles or formal roles — they are patterns of where a senior engineer concentrates attention and what kind of impact they produce.
The
Tech Lead
works closely with one team, guiding technical decisions, running design reviews, and helping engineers grow. The
Architect
owns the technical direction for a domain or platform, sets cross-team standards, and ensures long-term system coherence without necessarily writing most of the code. The
Solver
is assigned the organization's hardest unsolved problems, works wherever the problem is, and moves on once it is resolved. The
Right Hand
operates as an extension of a senior executive's (VP or CTO) bandwidth, representing that leader's technical judgment across the organization.

Most Staff engineers operate primarily in one archetype at any given company, though the mix shifts as organizations change and as individuals grow. These are not rigid boxes — many engineers blend two, and a single person may shift archetype when they change companies.
Why This Framework Matters

Without a clear model of archetypes, organizations hire Staff engineers for one pattern and deploy them in another — then wonder why they underperform. A Tech Lead archetype dropped into a pure architecture role without a team to guide will feel disconnected and lose impact. A Solver parachuted into a stable team and asked to stick around indefinitely will find the work unstimulating and the organization frustrated by their reluctance to settle in.

From the individual's side, not recognizing your own archetype leads to comparing yourself against the wrong peer set. A Staff engineer who is a natural Solver should not measure success by architecture RFC production; a Tech Lead should not feel inadequate for rarely working outside their team's boundaries. Naming the archetype lets each engineer define success in terms appropriate to how they actually create value.

For engineering managers and VPs, the archetype framework is a staffing and scoping tool. It makes explicit what kind of Staff-level help the organization needs next: another Tech Lead to staff a new team, an Architect to bring coherence to a fragmented microservices estate, a Solver to untangle a critical reliability problem, or a Right Hand to scale the CTO's reach during rapid growth.
Benefits of Using the Archetype Model
Reduces mismatch between expectations and reality.
Naming the archetype before hiring or promoting gives both sides a common vocabulary for what success looks like in the role.
Guides career self-assessment.
Engineers can evaluate past work against the archetype most natural to them, then deliberately develop the skills that archetype requires rather than trying to excel at all things equally.
Improves organizational staffing decisions.
Instead of filling generic "Staff Engineer" headcount, organizations can diagnose what kind of senior technical capacity is actually missing and hire accordingly.
Normalizes a wider range of valuable contributions.

Without the framework, organizations tend to favor a single mental model of what a senior engineer does. The archetype model validates that deep team embedding, broad architectural influence, and roving problem-solving are all legitimate and valuable paths.
Supports honest performance conversations.
A manager can evaluate a Tech Lead on team-level technical quality and engineer growth rather than penalizing them for not producing org-wide architecture documents.
Trade-offs and Limitations
The archetype model is a descriptive framework, not a prescriptive one, and it has important limitations to keep in mind.
Archetypes are not equally available at every company.

Most early-stage startups have no room for an Architect archetype — the organization is too small and moving too fast. An engineer who thrives as an Architect may have to operate as a Tech Lead or Solver until the company reaches the scale where the Architect role exists.
The Right Hand archetype is fragile.

Its effectiveness depends heavily on the relationship with the specific leader it supports. If that leader leaves, is reorganized, or changes their own priorities, the Right Hand's role can disappear or become undefined overnight.
Archetype labels can be misused.

Managers sometimes use the framework to cap expectations — telling a Solver "we don't need you involved in architecture," when in fact the best Solvers do whatever the problem demands, including architectural thinking.
No archetype has a monopoly on coding.

All four archetypes involve technical depth. The misconception that Architects or Right Hands primarily write documents while only Tech Leads and Solvers write code is false and harmful — credibility in all four archetypes depends on continuing to do technical work.
Self-identification can be aspirational rather than accurate.
Engineers often identify with the archetype they want to be rather than the one they naturally are. Feedback from managers, peers, and reports is a more reliable source than introspection alone.
How Each Archetype Works
The Tech Lead

The Tech Lead is the most common archetype, present in virtually every engineering organization of any size. They are embedded within a single team — usually the largest or most strategically important one in their org — and are responsible for the team's technical quality, velocity, and direction.

A Tech Lead attends sprint planning to identify design risks early, writes the most architecturally consequential code, reviews PRs for systemic concerns rather than style, runs architectural design sessions when new features require significant decisions, and acts as a technical voice alongside the product manager. They track technical debt, propose paydown strategies, and set the engineering standards the team operates under.

Critically, the Tech Lead operates through the team rather than around it. A Tech Lead who makes every technical decision unilaterally will slow the team down and stunt the growth of junior engineers. The most effective Tech Leads spend as much energy on raising the team's collective technical capability as on making the right call in the moment.
The Architect

The Architect archetype owns the technical vision for a domain — a platform, a product area, or a technical discipline (data infrastructure, security, APIs). Their mandate is long-term system coherence: ensuring that the decisions made by dozens of teams over months and years add up to something that remains operable and evolvable rather than a patchwork of local optimizations.

Architects produce technical strategies, write RFCs that define org-wide standards, sit in on design reviews across multiple teams to catch cross-cutting inconsistencies, and invest in the shared libraries and platforms that make local teams' work easier. They rarely own a single team's roadmap — instead, they shape the constraints and defaults within which many teams operate.

The main challenge for Architects is maintaining influence without authority. They cannot mandate that teams adopt their designs — they must persuade. This requires deep domain credibility (so engineers trust their judgment), strong written communication (so their proposals are legible to people who weren't in the room), and the political skill to navigate competing team priorities.
The Solver

The Solver is dispatched to the organization's most difficult, ambiguous, and high-stakes problems. These might be a critical database migration that has resisted multiple previous attempts, a security vulnerability that requires coordinated changes across many services, a performance crisis that is costing revenue, or a new technical capability that nobody else in the company yet knows how to build.

Solvers move from problem to problem rather than settling permanently in one team. This is both their strength and a source of friction: they accumulate diverse experience rapidly and become skilled at getting up to speed in unfamiliar codebases, but they may be perceived as parachuting in, changing things, and leaving without understanding the team's full context. The best Solvers are explicit about their temporary nature, involve the team deeply in the solution, and take care to ensure the team understands and can maintain the outcome after they leave.

Organizations that are scaling rapidly or dealing with significant accumulated technical debt have strong demand for Solvers. Organizations in a steady operational phase may not — there, a permanent Solver has no obvious home, and may feel underutilized or drifting.
The Right Hand

The Right Hand archetype is the rarest and most senior-adjacent. This engineer operates as an extension of a specific senior leader — typically a VP of Engineering or CTO — providing technical judgment, decision-making capacity, and presence in contexts the leader cannot personally cover.

Where a CTO cannot attend every technical design review, the Right Hand represents their perspective. Where the VP needs technical credibility in a vendor negotiation, the Right Hand provides it. Where an org-wide initiative needs a technical owner who can operate with the authority of the executive sponsor, the Right Hand fills that role. The work itself varies enormously, because it is determined by what the leader needs, not by a fixed domain.

The risk is dependence. The Right Hand's effectiveness is bounded by their relationship with a specific person. If that leader changes, the Right Hand may find their informal authority diminished before a new relationship is established. Right Hands who thrive in the long term build enough of their own reputation and relationships that they are not purely dependent on the leader they support.
Architecture: The Four Archetypes at a Glance
The diagram below shows the four archetypes side by side, highlighting each one's scope, mode of impact, and primary lever for creating value.

attrs.alt: The four Staff engineer archetypes: Tech Lead, Architect, Solver, and Right Hand — showing each one's scope, mode, and primary lever

content.text: Two dimensions most clearly differentiate the archetypes:
scope
(who they primarily serve — a single team vs. the broader organization) and
mode

(whether impact comes primarily from execution or from design and strategy). The Tech Lead is team-scoped and execution-oriented. The Architect is org-scoped and design-oriented. The Solver is org-scoped and execution-oriented. The Right Hand is org-scoped but driven by leadership adjacency rather than either dimension alone.
When to Use Each Archetype
The right archetype for a given engineer or a given role depends on what the organization needs and what the individual does best. These signals help identify the fit.
Use the Tech Lead archetype when
A team needs a steady technical anchor — consistent code quality, design process, and engineering standards across many sprints.
There is a strong junior or mid-level team that would benefit most from close mentorship and visible senior technical work.
The engineer draws energy from close collaboration, relationship-building, and seeing a team grow over time.
Use the Architect archetype when
Multiple teams are making overlapping and potentially conflicting technical decisions in a shared domain, and coherence is at risk.
The organization is large enough that informal coordination is insufficient and explicit standards, RFCs, and shared platforms are needed.
The engineer has deep breadth across a domain and strong writing and influencing skills.
Use the Solver archetype when
A specific critical problem is blocking the company and has resisted previous attempts or requires deeper expertise than any existing team possesses.
The organization is in a growth or crisis phase where a new category of hard technical problem is appearing frequently.
The engineer is energized by variety, ambiguity, and the intellectual challenge of unfamiliar problem spaces.
Use the Right Hand archetype when
A senior leader is stretched across too many technical decisions and needs a trusted technical counterpart.
Org-level technical decisions are being made without technical credibility in the room and errors are compounding.
The engineer has broad technical range, strong organizational instincts, and the communication skills to represent a leader's perspective faithfully.
The decision flow diagram below provides a structured way to identify which archetype best fits a given situation.

attrs.alt: Decision flow for choosing a Staff engineer archetype: Tech Lead, Architect, Solver, or Right Hand

content.text: How to Embody Your Archetype
Tech Lead in Practice

A Tech Lead's weekly rhythm centers on the team's work. They review design documents before significant implementation begins, identify technical risks in sprint planning, and code actively enough to retain credibility and catch real issues in review. They hold office hours or pairing sessions to unblock engineers and accelerate learning. They communicate the team's technical status to stakeholders — including escalating when technical debt or dependencies will affect delivery — without passing that complexity onto every individual engineer.

Tech Lead weekly touchpoints (example):

 Monday
   Sprint planning — identify technical risks in new tickets
   Sync with PM on scope and feasibility of upcoming work

 Tuesday–Thursday
   Code review with architectural lens (not line-by-line style)
   Pair with engineers on blocked tasks
   Write or review design docs for features in flight

 Friday
   Retrospective — collect technical improvement items
   Update team tech health notes: debt, dependencies, blockers
   Review monitoring / alerts for systemic patterns
Architect in Practice

An Architect's calendar looks different: fewer sprint standups, more cross-team design reviews. They invest significant time in documentation — proposals, standards, threat models, runbooks — because influence at scale requires written reasoning that outlasts any meeting. They make it a priority to understand what teams are building before those teams make irreversible decisions, which often means proactive communication ("I heard you're starting the API redesign — can I join the design session?") rather than waiting to be invited.

Architect output types (illustrative):

 RFC: "Event schema versioning standard for data platform v2"
   — defines breaking vs. non-breaking changes
   — specifies backward compatibility requirement windows
   — links to migration tooling
   — opens for 2-week comment period across 6 teams

 ADR (Architecture Decision Record): "Why we chose gRPC over REST for internal services"
   — documents context, alternatives considered, decision, consequences
   — referenced by new teams when making the same decision later

 Technical strategy memo: "Database layer 2024 roadmap"
   — current state (honest), target state (concrete), sequenced milestones
Solver in Practice

When a Solver joins a problem, their first investment is understanding. They read code they did not write, instrument systems they have not seen before, and talk to engineers who have context they lack. This diagnosis phase — which can feel uncomfortably slow — is what distinguishes a Solver from an engineer who jumps to solutions before they understand the system. The Solver's real skill is not having all the answers, but getting to the right understanding faster than anyone else.

Solver engagement phases (example for a reliability problem):

 Week 1: Observe and instrument
   — Add tracing / metrics to understand failure modes
   — Read recent incident reports and postmortems
   — Interview on-call engineers about patterns they've noticed
   — Map dependencies that could be contributing

 Week 2-3: Root cause and prototype
   — Isolate contributing factors with targeted experiments
   — Implement a minimal fix and test in staging
   — Document findings with enough detail for others to verify

 Week 4+: Implement, validate, document, hand off
   — Ship fix with rollout plan
   — Write runbook for future operators
   — Conduct knowledge transfer session with team
   — Exit; check in once after 30 days
Right Hand in Practice

The Right Hand must develop clarity with their leader about decision authority. Which decisions can the Right Hand make on the leader's behalf? Which require explicit sign-off? In which contexts should the Right Hand speak first and escalate later vs. always escalating? This operating agreement — ideally written down — prevents the most common failure mode of the Right Hand: overreaching in a context where the leader expected to be consulted, or under-reaching and failing to exercise the delegated authority they were given.

Right Hand operating agreement (example):

 Delegated decisions (Right Hand decides, informs leader):
   — Vendor evaluation shortlisting (leader selects final)
   — Technical interview bar decisions for Staff+ candidates
   — Architectural guidance in ad-hoc design reviews

 Escalate before deciding:
   — Budget commitments above $50K
   — Organizational process changes
   — Decisions that affect more than 3 teams
   — Anything that will be announced publicly

 Weekly sync: 30 min, Friday — align on next week's priorities and any boundary cases
Examples
Example 1: Tech Lead in a product team

Priya is a Staff Engineer on the Payments team at a mid-sized e-commerce company. Her team of six engineers (two senior, four mid-level) owns checkout, refunds, and reconciliation. She reviews every design document before implementation, participates in sprint planning to spot over-scoped stories early, and writes code on the critical path — most recently the new tokenization layer for the PCI-compliant card vault. She pairs with junior engineers weekly, holds biweekly tech-debt sessions where the team votes on paydown priorities, and writes a monthly "Technical State of Payments" note for the engineering director. She does not attend meetings outside the Payments team unless a cross-team dependency directly affects her team's work.
Example 2: Solver on a migration crisis

Santiago is a Staff Engineer who operates as a Solver at a logistics company. The company's legacy Oracle database cannot handle the order volume projected for the upcoming holiday season. Four previous attempts over two years to migrate to PostgreSQL have stalled. Santiago is assigned to the problem with a six-month mandate. He spends the first three weeks not writing a single line of migration code — instead, he maps the schema dependencies, audits the previous attempts to understand why they failed, and instruments the application to know exactly which queries are performance-critical. He discovers that 80% of the migration risk is concentrated in three stored procedures used by the core order-placement flow. He redesigns the migration strategy to address those three procedures first, ships a working pilot, and uses the pilot's success to build team confidence for the remaining work. Six months later, the migration is complete and he moves to the company's next critical problem: an overloaded search infrastructure.
Real-world Example: Archetypes at Scale

At a company like Stripe, which has grown from 50 to thousands of engineers over a decade, all four archetypes exist simultaneously and serve distinct organizational functions. Tech Leads are the backbone of every product area team — they own technical quality in billing, radar, connect, and dozens of other products. Architects exist at the platform level: engineers who own the API design philosophy, the data model for financial objects, and the event system that connects dozens of services. When Stripe launched a new product category (like banking infrastructure), Solvers were assigned to design the new system's foundations, figure out the regulatory and technical constraints from scratch, and hand off to a stable team once the patterns were established. The Right Hand archetype appears at the Director of Infrastructure level, where Staff engineers extend the VPs' technical reach into acquisition due diligence, cross-functional regulatory projects, and industry standards bodies.

The organizational lesson from companies at this scale is that you need all four. A company that only promotes Tech Leads will have excellent team-level quality but fragmented system architecture as scale increases. A company that creates only Architects risks abstraction without execution. A company with only Solvers never builds a steady foundation. Getting the mix right — and knowing which archetype a given person thrives as — is a core competency of senior engineering leadership.
Common Mistakes
Assuming all Staff engineers should operate the same way

The most pervasive mistake is treating "Staff Engineer" as a single role description. Organizations write one career ladder definition and expect all Staff engineers to meet it, regardless of archetype. The result is that Solvers are penalized for not attending team standups, Tech Leads are penalized for not producing architecture RFCs, and everyone is measured against a composite standard that no single person can fully embody. Effective organizations define expectations by archetype, not just by level.
Forcing a mismatch between archetype and context

Placing an Architect archetype in a five-person startup is a common mismatch. The organization is too small and fast-moving for the level of process, documentation, and cross-team coordination the Architect archetype requires. The person will either adapt into a Tech Lead role (which works) or try to impose premature architecture (which slows the company). Recognizing that context determines which archetype is viable is more important than assessing what archetype a person prefers.
Thinking the Right Hand only does soft work

The Right Hand archetype is sometimes reduced to "Chief of Staff" with a technical title. In reality, the most effective Right Hands do substantial technical work — they write code, design systems, review infrastructure decisions, and produce technical artifacts. The leadership adjacency does not replace technical depth; it amplifies it by giving that depth access to org-wide scope and decision-making authority.
Treating archetypes as permanent labels

An engineer who was a Solver at their previous company may need to operate as a Tech Lead at their new one, because that is what the new context requires. Archetype fit depends on organizational need, career stage, personal energy, and what the specific company can support. Treating an archetype as a fixed identity prevents the necessary flexibility.
Best Practices
Make the archetype explicit in job descriptions.

When hiring or defining a role, state the primary archetype in the job description rather than listing a generic set of Staff engineer responsibilities. This reduces candidate mismatch and sets clearer expectations.
Align on archetype at the start of a new role.

Staff engineers joining a new team or company should have an explicit conversation with their manager in the first 30 days: which archetype does the organization need from me here? Document the answer and revisit it every six months.
Develop breadth in your secondary archetype.

Archetypes blend and transition over time. Tech Leads who develop Architect skills can expand their scope without changing companies. Solvers who develop Tech Lead skills can take on stable team ownership when the organization needs it.
For managers: evaluate by archetype, not against a single template.
Performance reviews should ask "Is this person excelling in the archetype they are operating in?" not "Are they doing all the things a composite Staff engineer should do?"
Plan for archetype transitions during org changes.

When a company moves from growth to efficiency mode, Solver demand often drops and Tech Lead demand rises. Proactively communicating these shifts to Staff engineers prevents disengagement and attrition.
Performance and Scalability of Each Archetype
Each archetype scales differently as the organization grows, and each has a ceiling beyond which its natural pattern of working stops being sufficient.
The
Tech Lead

archetype scales linearly: one Tech Lead per team. As the engineering organization grows from 20 to 200 engineers, the company needs roughly proportionally more Tech Leads. The archetype itself does not become less effective, but an individual Tech Lead's direct impact does not scale beyond their one team without transitioning toward the Architect pattern.
The
Architect

archetype scales superlinearly in potential impact: a single Architect who shapes a platform standard used by 20 teams has multiplied their output by a factor of 20. The constraint is that the influence model — writing, reviewing, persuading — does not scale beyond a certain cognitive load. Most Architects can effectively own one or two major domains; spreading across too many loses coherence.
The
Solver

archetype is demand-constrained: in a company with few hard unsolved problems, there is no room for a Solver. In a company undergoing rapid change, there may be demand for many Solvers. The bottleneck is problem identification — someone must decide which problems are hard enough to warrant a Solver, and someone must ensure the Solver is actually working on those problems and not on comfortable work.
The
Right Hand

archetype scales with the seniority and span of the leader it supports. A Right Hand for a VP of Engineering with 100 reports has a large effective footprint. If the VP grows to 300 reports, the Right Hand's scope grows proportionally. The ceiling is personal: if the leader becomes so senior that their Right Hand cannot keep up technically, the relationship breaks down.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Several books and frameworks support applying the archetype model in practice.
Staff Engineer: Leadership Beyond the Management Track
(Will Larson, 2021) — the source of the four archetype framework. Includes interview transcripts with Staff engineers across industry, illustrating each archetype in practice.
The Staff Engineer's Path
(Tanya Reilly, 2022) — complements Larson's book with practical guidance on navigating the organizational dynamics of each archetype.
StaffEng.com
— a community-maintained resource with Staff engineer stories, role descriptions, and career advice organized by archetype.
Engineering career ladders

— public engineering ladders from companies like Dropbox, Etsy, and Rent the Runway have begun incorporating archetype language into Staff+ level descriptions. Reviewing these helps calibrate expectations.
RFC and ADR templates

— Architect archetype engineers benefit from established RFC processes. The IETF RFC format, lightweight ADR (Architecture Decision Record) templates from the adr-tools project, and Amazon's six-pager format are all worth studying depending on the organization's size and culture.
Comparison with Related Frameworks
The archetype model is one of several frameworks for thinking about senior individual contributor roles. The table below compares the key alternatives.
Framework
Core concept
Strength
Limitation
Larson archetypes
4 primary patterns of where a Staff engineer focuses
Empirically grounded; maps to real organizational needs
Does not specify skills or behaviors within each archetype
T-shaped / π-shaped skills
Deep expertise in one (or two) areas + broad breadth
Describes individual skill profile clearly
Doesn't capture organizational role or impact pattern
Maker vs. Multiplier
Direct output (Maker) vs. enabling others' output (Multiplier)
Useful mental model for leverage and delegation
Binary; ignores organizational scope dimension
DORA metrics / IC effectiveness
Measures outcomes: deployment frequency, lead time, MTTR
Quantifiable; grounded in measurable team performance
Measures team output; hard to attribute to one engineer
Security and Reliability Risks
Each archetype carries specific organizational risks when used incorrectly or without adequate structure.
Tech Lead single point of failure.

When a Tech Lead accumulates too much organizational knowledge and too many decision rights, the team becomes dangerously dependent. If the Tech Lead leaves, the team can lose months of context. Mitigating this requires active knowledge distribution: written documentation, decision records, and deliberate knowledge transfer to senior engineers on the team.
Architect creating ivory-tower standards.

An Architect who sets standards without understanding the practical constraints teams face will produce compliance theater: teams formally adopt the standards and then work around them in practice. The mitigation is mandatory prototyping — Architects should implement their own standards in at least one production context before declaring them broadly applicable.
Solver leaving incomplete work.

A Solver who resolves the immediate crisis and moves on without ensuring the team understands the solution creates a time-bomb: the next time the system exhibits the same failure, the team has no internal knowledge to draw on. Structured handoff — written runbooks, knowledge transfer sessions, and a 30-day check-in after departure — is not optional.
Right Hand losing technical grounding.

The Right Hand who spends too much time in leadership meetings and too little time in code, systems, and technical conversations gradually loses the technical credibility that makes the archetype effective. Maintaining regular technical work — even a few hours per week in a codebase, reviewing infrastructure changes, or participating in design sessions — is necessary to prevent this drift.
Mental Model
Think of the four archetypes as different roles on a sports team, where the team is the engineering organization.
The
Tech Lead

is the team captain on the field: present at every play, setting the tone, leading by example in execution, and developing the players around them. Their impact is measured in how well their specific team performs.
The
Architect
is the game strategist and playbook designer: they shape the rules under which many teams operate. Their impact is measured in how cohesively the whole league plays.
The
Solver

is the specialist brought in for a specific problem — the kicker in American football, the specialist bowler in cricket: called into the game for a specific high-stakes moment, makes a decisive contribution, and steps back when the moment has passed.
The
Right Hand

is the general manager's trusted technical advisor: not the coach, not the player, but the person who makes sure the leadership's technical instincts are well-informed and well-executed across the entire franchise.

In all four cases, the sport — deep technical excellence — is still the foundation. The archetypes describe who the athlete plays alongside and what game situation they are optimized for, not whether they train.
Key Takeaways
The four Staff engineer archetypes —
Tech Lead, Architect, Solver, and Right Hand
— describe distinct patterns of where a senior individual contributor focuses their work and how they create organizational value.
The
Tech Lead
is team-embedded and execution-oriented; the
Architect
is cross-org and design-oriented; the
Solver
is cross-org and problem-solving-oriented; the
Right Hand
is leadership-adjacent and org-scope.
Archetype fit depends on both the
individual's natural strengths
and the
organization's current needs
; not every archetype exists in every context.
Mismatching archetype to context — placing an Architect in a startup, or a Solver in a stable team — is a leading cause of Staff engineer underperformance that has nothing to do with capability.
All four archetypes require sustained
technical depth
; the archetype determines the organizational scope of impact, not whether an engineer writes code.
Organizations should evaluate Staff engineers
by archetype
rather than against a single composite template that no individual person can fully embody.
Archetypes are not permanent: engineers shift between them across companies and career stages, and deliberately developing a secondary archetype expands long-term career optionality.
